stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s]] & [[t]] \\ [[p]] & [[m22]] & [[m23]] \\ [[q]] & [[m32]] & [[m33]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is \( \dim(\operatorname{Col}(A)) \), the dimension of the column space of \( A \)?
solution: \( \dim(\operatorname{Col}(A)) \) equals \( \operatorname{rank}(A) \). Row-reducing \( A \) gives rank \( [[answer]] \), so \( \dim(\operatorname{Col}(A)) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \dim(\operatorname{Null}(A)) = 3-\operatorname{rank}(A) \), not the column space dimension.
This overcounts the rank by one.
This undercounts the rank by one.
This overcounts the rank, which cannot exceed the matrix size.
This assumes \(A\) is the zero matrix.
This assumes \(A\) is invertible without checking for dependence among its rows.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s]] & [[t]] \\ [[p]] & [[m22]] & [[m23]] \\ [[q]] & [[m32]] & [[m33]] \end{bmatrix} \). What is \( \dim(\operatorname{Null}(A)) \), the dimension of the null space of \( A \)?
distractor_rationales: This is \( \dim(\operatorname{Col}(A)) = \operatorname{rank}(A) \), not the null space dimension.
This assumes \(A\) is the zero matrix, so the null space would be all of \( \mathbb{R}^3 \).
This overcounts the free columns by one.
This undercounts the free columns by one.
This is a plausible but unverified nullity value.
This is a plausible but unverified nullity value.
This assumes \(A\) is invertible, giving a trivial null space.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & [[s]] & [[t]] \\ [[p]] & [[m22]] & [[m23]] \\ [[q]] & [[m32]] & [[m33]] \\ [[v4a]] & [[v4b]] & [[v4c]] \end{bmatrix} \), a \(4\times3\) matrix. What is \( \dim(\operatorname{Null}(A^{T})) \), the dimension of the left null space of \( A \)?
solution: The left null space \( \operatorname{Null}(A^{T}) \) has dimension \( m - \operatorname{rank}(A) \), where \( m=4 \) is the number of rows of \( A \). Row-reducing \( A \) shows \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[rankval]] \), so \( \dim(\operatorname{Null}(A^{T})) = 4 - [[rankval]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \operatorname{rank}(A) \) itself, not the left null space dimension.
This assumes \(A\) is the zero matrix, giving the largest possible left null space.
This overcounts the dimension by one.
This undercounts the dimension by one.
This is a plausible but unverified value.
This is a plausible but unverified value.
This assumes \(A\) has full row rank, leaving no left null space.

stem: A matrix \( A \) is \( [[m]] \times [[n]] \) (mapping \( \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \to \mathbb{R}^{[[m]]} \)) with \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[r]] \). Since the row space and the null space of \(A\) are orthogonal complements within \( \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \), what is \( \dim(\operatorname{Null}(A)) \)?
solution: Since \( \operatorname{Row}(A) \) and \( \operatorname{Null}(A) \) are orthogonal complements in \( \mathbb{R}^{[[n]]} \), their dimensions add to \( n \): \( \dim(\operatorname{Null}(A)) = [[n]] - \dim(\operatorname{Row}(A)) = [[n]] - [[r]] = [[answer]] \) (using \( \dim(\operatorname{Row}(A)) = \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[r]] \)).
distractor_rationales: This uses the codomain dimension \(m\) instead of the domain dimension \(n\) (that formula gives the left null space dimension instead).
This reports the rank itself instead of subtracting it from \(n\).
This subtracts the two given dimensions instead of using the rank.
This adds instead of subtracting.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This is off by one from the correct dimension.
This assumes the rank is \(0\), reporting the codomain dimension.
This assumes the rank is \(0\), reporting the full domain dimension.
